title: Anticancer Drugs (Part 1): Antimetabolites & Antibiotics
summary: الادوية المضادة للسرطان تعمل على ايقاف انقسام الخلايا السرطانية. تنقسم الى ادوية خاصة بدورة الخلية (CCS) مثل مضادات الاستقلاب والانتيبيوتيك، واخرى غير خاصة (CCNS). مضادات الاستقلاب تشمل الميثوتريكسات و6-ميركابتوبيورين و5-فلورويوراسيل وكلها تتداخل مع تركيب الحمض النووي. الانتيبيوتيك السرطانية مثل البليوميسين والدوكسوروبيسين تعمل مباشرة على الDNA ولكن لكل منها سمية خاصة تميزه.

key_drugs.name: Methotrexate (MTX)
key_drugs.note: يثبط DHFR، يُنقذ منه باعطاء Leucovorin

key_drugs.name: 5-Fluorouracil (5-FU)
key_drugs.note: يثبط Thymidylate Synthase، يُعطى مع Leucovorin

key_drugs.name: Bleomycin
key_drugs.note: السمية الرئوية هي الحد المقيِّد للجرعة

key_drugs.name: Doxorubicin
key_drugs.note: سمية قلبية لا رجعة فيها، يحميها Dexrazoxane

mnemonics.title: MAP = مضادات الاستقلاب الثلاثة
mnemonics.body:
M = Methotrexate (فولات)
A = 6-MP Analog (بيورين)
P = Pyrimidine = 5-FU
كلها CCS تضرب الDNA

mnemonics.title: ميثوتريكسات والانقاذ
mnemonics.body: تخيّل ان MTX هو لص يسرق مفتاح DHFR فتتوقف المصنع (DNA). الانقاذ = Leucovorin يعطي المصنع مفتاح بديل من الباب الخلفي.

mnemonics.title: 6-MP والAllopurinol: خطر التراكم
mnemonics.body: Allopurinol يكسر Xanthine Oxidase الذي يهضم 6-MP. اذا اعطيتهما معا، 6-MP يتراكم في الدم كالماء خلف سد مغلق - السمية تضاعفت! خفّض الجرعة.

mnemonics.title: قلب الدوكسوروبيسين الاحمر
mnemonics.body: تخيّل قلبا احمر اللون (البول يصبح احمر) تحته شرارات كهربائية (Free Radicals) تحرق عضلة القلب. Dexrazoxane = درع حديدي يحمي القلب من الشرار.

mnemonics.title: BLOW = سمية البليوميسين
mnemonics.body:
B = Bleomycin
L = Lungs (سمية رئوية رئيسية)
O = OK for bone marrow (نادر ضغط النخاع)
W = Wounds/Skin (تغيرات جلدية)
الرئة هي نقطة الضعف!

mnemonics.title: Chemo Man — سُميّات العلاج الكيميائي
mnemonics.body: خريطة الجسم: الرأس/الأعصاب N = Nitrosoureas (عصبي، يعبر BBB). العيون/الأذن C = Cisplatin/Carboplatin (نيفرو/أوتو). القلب D = Doxorubicin/Daunorubicin (سُمّية قلبية). الصدر B = Bleomycin/Busulfan (تليف رئوي). البطن MF = Methotrexate + 5-FU (كبت نقي العظم). المثانة P = cycloPhosphamide (التهاب مثانة نزفي → اعطِ Mesna). الأطراف V = Vinca alkaloids (اعتلال أعصاب طرفي). Tamoxifen → تغيّر حدّة الإبصار.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between CCS and CCNS?
flashcards.back: CCS (Cell Cycle Specific) = acts only on dividing cells, e.g., antimetabolites and Bleomycin. CCNS = acts on both resting and dividing cells, e.g., alkylating agents.

flashcards.front: What is the mechanism of action of Methotrexate?
flashcards.back: Inhibits the enzyme Dihydrofolate Reductase (DHFR), blocking conversion of FH2 to FH4, thereby preventing synthesis of DNA, RNA, and protein.

flashcards.front: What is Leucovorin Rescue?
flashcards.back: Administration of Leucovorin (Folinic Acid) after high-dose MTX to bypass the inhibited enzyme and rescue normal cells from death.

flashcards.front: Why must the dose of 6-MP be reduced when taking Allopurinol?
flashcards.back: Allopurinol inhibits Xanthine Oxidase, which normally breaks down 6-MP, causing the drug to accumulate and its toxicity to increase — reduce the dose by 25–75%.

flashcards.front: What is the distinctive skin toxicity of 5-FU?
flashcards.back: Hand-Foot Syndrome: redness and peeling of the palms and soles following prolonged infusion.

flashcards.front: Why is Leucovorin given with 5-FU?
flashcards.back: Because Leucovorin stabilizes the binding of FdUMP to Thymidylate Synthase, enhancing the efficacy of 5-FU.

flashcards.front: What is the dose-limiting toxicity of Bleomycin?
flashcards.back: Pulmonary toxicity: ranging from cough to fatal pulmonary fibrosis.

flashcards.front: What is the mechanism of action of Doxorubicin?
flashcards.back: Intercalates between DNA strands and inhibits Topoisomerase II, causing strand breaks and generation of free radicals.

flashcards.front: What drug protects against Doxorubicin cardiotoxicity?
flashcards.back: Dexrazoxane (an iron chelator) reduces free radical generation. Liposomal encapsulation also reduces cardiac toxicity.

flashcards.front: What color is the urine with Doxorubicin?
flashcards.back: Red (due to the drug's own pigment — unrelated to blood).

flashcards.front: What are the common side effects shared by most anticancer drugs?
flashcards.back: Nausea and vomiting, diarrhea, stomatitis (mouth sores), bone marrow suppression (myelodepression), and hair loss (alopecia).

flashcards.front: What are the non-oncologic uses of MTX?
flashcards.back: At low doses: treatment of Rheumatoid Arthritis and Psoriasis.

flashcards.front: What is MDR in cancer?
flashcards.back: Multiple Drug Resistance = resistance of cancer cells to more than one drug simultaneously.

flashcards.front: What is POMP?
flashcards.back: A combination chemotherapy protocol for leukemia comprising: Prednisone, Oncovin (Vincristine), Methotrexate, and Purinethol (6-MP).

flashcards.front: Why are anticancer drugs given intermittently?
flashcards.back: To allow normal cells to recover between doses while maintaining the effect on cancer cells.

flashcards.front: What is the renal toxicity of MTX and how is it prevented?
flashcards.back: At high doses it causes kidney damage — prevented by adequate hydration and urinary alkalinization.

flashcards.front: What increases Doxorubicin cardiotoxicity?
flashcards.back: Thoracic irradiation (Thorax Irradiation) and the monoclonal antibody Trastuzumab.

flashcards.front: What cell-cycle category does Bleomycin belong to?
flashcards.back: CCS — Cell Cycle Specific.

flashcards.front: What is meant by cell cycle specificity?
flashcards.back: Cell cycle-specific (CCS) drugs kill at a defined phase (antimetabolites, vinca alkaloids); non-specific (CCNS) drugs kill at any phase (alkylating agents).

flashcards.front: Why are rapidly dividing tumors more sensitive to chemotherapy?
flashcards.back: Because most drugs target dividing cells; resting G0 cells are more resistant.

flashcards.front: What is the mechanism of alkylating agents?
flashcards.back: Transfer alkyl groups to the N7 position of guanine → strand breaks and cross-linking of DNA chains; mutagenic and carcinogenic (CCNS).

flashcards.front: What is the distinctive toxicity of Cyclophosphamide?
flashcards.back: Hemorrhagic cystitis caused by acrolein → prevented by Mesna and hydration.

flashcards.front: What is the toxicity of Cisplatin?
flashcards.back: Nephrotoxicity, ototoxicity, and severe nausea (give ondansetron + hydration).

flashcards.front: What is the mechanism of Methotrexate and its rescue?
flashcards.back: Inhibits dihydrofolate reductase; rescued with Leucovorin (folinic acid).

flashcards.front: What is the mechanism of 5-Fluorouracil?
flashcards.back: Pyrimidine analogue that inhibits thymidylate synthase; causes bone marrow suppression and diarrhea.

flashcards.front: Why is combination (multi-drug) therapy used?
flashcards.back: To increase cell kill, reduce resistance, and distribute toxicity across different organs.

mcqs.q: Which of the following is used to rescue a patient from high-dose Methotrexate?
mcqs.explanation: Leucovorin (Folinic Acid) bypasses the inhibited DHFR enzyme and restores folate synthesis in normal cells.

mcqs.q: A patient taking 6-Mercaptopurine is started on Allopurinol for gout. What should be done?
options: There is no interaction between them
mcqs.explanation: Allopurinol inhibits Xanthine Oxidase, which normally metabolizes 6-MP, raising its level and toxicity. The solution is to reduce the dose.

mcqs.q: What is the dose-limiting toxicity of Bleomycin?
mcqs.explanation: Pulmonary toxicity of Bleomycin ranges from cough to fatal pulmonary fibrosis and is the primary dose-limiting factor.

mcqs.q: The cardiotoxic mechanism of Doxorubicin depends on:
options: Free radical generation via Topoisomerase II inhibition
mcqs.explanation: Free radicals produced by Topoisomerase II inhibition cause single-strand breaks in cardiac muscle cell DNA.

mcqs.q: Which of these drugs is used at low doses to treat Rheumatoid Arthritis?
mcqs.explanation: MTX at low doses has an anti-inflammatory effect used in Rheumatoid Arthritis and Psoriasis.

mcqs.q: What is the interaction between 5-FU and Leucovorin?
options: Leucovorin stabilizes 5-FU binding to Thymidylate Synthase, enhancing its efficacy
mcqs.explanation: Leucovorin as a co-factor stabilizes the FdUMP–Thymidylate Synthase complex, increasing inhibition efficacy.

mcqs.q: Hand-Foot Syndrome is a distinctive side effect of:
mcqs.explanation: 5-FU following prolonged infusion causes redness and peeling of the palms and soles (Hand-Foot Syndrome).

mcqs.q: CCS (Cell Cycle Specific) drugs include all of the following EXCEPT:
mcqs.explanation: Mechlorethamine is a CCNS alkylating agent that acts on both resting and dividing cells. All the others are CCS.

mcqs.q: What drug protects the heart from Doxorubicin toxicity?
mcqs.explanation: Dexrazoxane is an iron chelator that prevents free radical generation responsible for cardiotoxicity.
Cancer cells overcome MTX by increasing DHFR production so that the drug dose is insufficient to inhibit it.

mcqs.q: Prevention of hemorrhagic cystitis from Cyclophosphamide:
Leucovorin is used to rescue from toxicity of:
mcqs.explanation: Folinic acid bypasses DHF reductase inhibition.

high_yield.point: Cyclophosphamide → التهاب مثانة نزفي (acrolein) → الوقاية بـ Mesna والإماهة.
Cisplatin → سُمّية كلوية وسمعية وغثيان شديد → إماهة وamifostine.
Methotrexate يثبّط dihydrofolate reductase؛ الإنقاذ بـ Leucovorin (folinic acid).
الأدوية النوعية لدورة الخلية (antimetabolites, vinca) مقابل غير النوعية (alkylating).
